figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison of TSROUTER with existing routing methods. Unlike previous approaches that only select among models under a fixed modality, TSROUTER jointly performs modality and model routing while capturing both query-query and query-model interactions with fast inference and low memory.

Figure 1: Motivation analysis results. (a) Distribution of best-performing modality across 15 subtasks grouped by 4 task categories. (b) Breakdown of queries correctly answered by only the smallest model, both models, and only the largest model within each modality.

Figure 2: Overview of TSROUTER. Each query is paired with all modality–model combinations to collect performance and cost, forming the training data. Then, TSROUTER builds a heterogeneous graph from task, query, modality, and model descriptions, learns node representations, and scores candidates to route queries to the optimal modality–model pair.
Table 3: Performance and cost comparison of various baselines on 4 time series reasoning tasks. Bold and underline denote the best and second-best results.

Figure 5: Accuracy–cost trade-off under varying cost preference α. Each subplot corresponds to a task and overall accuracy. TSROUTER traces a clear Pareto front, consistently dominating all baselines across the full range of cost budgets.
Figure 7: Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis. (a) Number of HGT layers; (b) Embedding dimension; (c) Number of nearest neighbors k. The dashed line indicates the bestperforming baseline.
Table 9: LLMs and VLMs used in TSROUTER with costs per 1M tokens.
Figure 9: Case study from the Perception task.
Table 10: Reasoning Trace of each modality-model for the case in Figure 9
